title: Exception Handling Basics
learningObjectives: Contrast checked, unchecked, and error throwables with interview-ready examples
Implement try/catch/finally flows and explain execution order under return and throw
Use try-with-resources and describe suppressed exceptions from close failures
Design custom domain exceptions with context fields and stable messages
Write defensive parsers that translate NumberFormatException into controlled outcomes
Choose catch granularity that avoids silent failures and pointless retries

sections.title: Why disciplined exception handling protects users and operators
sections.content:
Exceptions are Java’s structured way to abort a failing operation, attach context, and unwind the stack until a handler can recover or translate the fault into a user-visible outcome. When teams misuse them for ordinary control flow or swallow errors with empty catches, production incidents look like silent data corruption instead of loud stack traces. Interviewers probe exception handling because it predicts whether you respect failure domains: what is recoverable, what is a bug, and what must never be caught.

Checked exceptions document expected failure modes the caller must acknowledge—think `IOException` when the network flakes. Unchecked `RuntimeException` types flag programmer mistakes such as `NullPointerException` or `IllegalArgumentException` that usually should not be caught in business logic. The `Error` hierarchy marks JVM-level faults like `OutOfMemoryError` that you should not try to handle as part of normal service flow. Confusing these categories leads to APIs that either force useless `throws` clauses everywhere or hide real bugs behind broad catches.

The `finally` block exists so cleanup runs whether control leaves via return, exception, or break—resources close, locks release, and metrics flush. Modern code prefers `try-with-resources` so the compiler wires `AutoCloseable` correctly and preserves suppressed exceptions from close failures. Misunderstanding `finally` shows up in connection leaks that only appear under load when rare paths skip your ad-hoc cleanup.

Custom exceptions give domain language to failures: `InsufficientStockException` beats a generic `RuntimeException("bad")` because callers can branch, metrics can tag, and logs read like the business story. Wrapping lower-level faults with causes preserves the original stack for operators while surfacing a stable message upstream. Parsing user input belongs in guarded `try` blocks that translate `NumberFormatException` into predictable sentinel values or validation errors instead of bubbling raw JVM wording to end users.

During on-call, exception hygiene determines mean time to recovery: chained causes let you jump from a service timeout to the socket reset that triggered it, while logging the wrong level or truncating stacks hides the smoking gun. Suppressed exceptions from `try-with-resources` explain double failures—write failing while close also fails—which otherwise look like ghosts in metrics. Alerting on swallowed exceptions is hard; prefer explicit handling or rethrow with context at boundaries. You will also see retry storms caused by catching broad `Exception` and assuming transient faults when the root cause was a deterministic `IllegalArgumentException` that will never succeed on retry, which is why classifying failures before handling them separates incident calm from incident amplification. Dashboards that only count HTTP 500s without inspecting exception classes will miss this pattern until queues back up and customers notice first.

This day trains you to narrate failures the way senior engineers do: name the category (checked, unchecked, error), show the `try/catch/finally` or `try-with-resources` shape, explain why you wrap causes, and finish with a test that proves cleanup always runs. When you mentor juniors, forbid empty catches except in documented shutdown hooks, and ask what observable signal remains when something goes wrong—if the answer is “nothing,” the handler is not finished yet. Pair every new catch with a logging or metrics decision: either the failure is actionable and should increment an error budget, or it should propagate until a boundary that knows how to degrade gracefully. Review boards care about `throws` clauses on public APIs because they encode contracts for integrators, and the assignment’s `safeParseInt` plus `InsufficientStockException` exercises mirror edge validation and domain-specific failure types you will defend in real reviews. Build the habit of reading stack traces from the bottom upward to find the original fault, then upward again to see which translation layers added context—interviewers notice when candidates do that instinctively during live debugging exercises. Treat every new catch as a documentation obligation: future readers should understand the failure policy in seconds, not hours.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — Throwable hierarchy, recovery, and cleanup
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

**Throwable** is the root of errors and exceptions in Java. **Exception** covers most application-level faults; **RuntimeException** marks unchecked failures; **Error** signals serious JVM problems. **Interview angle:** Never confuse recoverable IO faults with `OutOfMemoryError`.

### 1. Checked versus unchecked

Checked exceptions extend `Exception` but not `RuntimeException` and must be caught or declared. Unchecked extend `RuntimeException` and are optional to handle. **Interview angle:** Use checked for recoverable environmental failures callers should plan for.

### 2. try / catch / finally

`try` runs guarded code; `catch` matches throwable types; `finally` executes after `try` completes or an exception propagates, before the method returns. **Interview angle:** `finally` still runs after `return` in `try` unless the JVM exits.

### 3. Multi-catch and ordering

Catch blocks are ordered most specific to general; `catch (IOException | SQLException e)` shares one handler. **Interview angle:** Placing `Exception` first hides more specific catches—compile error.

### 4. try-with-resources

Auto-closes `AutoCloseable` instances in reverse order; primary exception is thrown while close exceptions attach as **suppressed**. **Interview angle:** Prefer over manual `finally` for resources.

### 5. Cause chains

`initCause` or throwable constructors with `cause` preserve root stacks when wrapping domain errors. **Interview angle:** Operators grep inner causes in logs.

### 6. Custom exceptions

Extend `Exception` for checked domain faults or `RuntimeException` for programmer rule violations; add fields for context. **Interview angle:** Name exceptions after business events, not generic `ServiceException` unless layered.

### 7. NumberFormatException

`Integer.parseInt` throws when digits invalid; catch locally for user input. **Interview angle:** Do not catch and ignore—return sentinel or validation error.

### 8. IllegalArgumentException

Signals bad parameters; typically unchecked. **Interview angle:** Validate early at API boundaries.

### 9. NullPointerException

Usually unchecked bug; avoid catching except at framework boundaries. **Interview angle:** Prefer `Objects.requireNonNull` for fast fail.

### 10. Stack traces and logging

Log full stack once at a boundary; avoid string concatenation that hides causes. **Interview angle:** Structured logging fields help metrics.

### 11. Swallowing exceptions

Empty `catch` blocks hide production defects; if truly ignorable, log at low level with justification. **Interview angle:** Code review red flag.

### 12. Rethrowing and translation

Service layer may translate `SQLException` to domain exception while retaining cause. **Interview angle:** Do not leak JDBC types to UI layers.

### 13. finally pitfalls

Returning from `finally` masks exceptions; avoid. **Interview angle:** Rare Java trivia question.

### 14. Errors

`Error` subclasses like `StackOverflowError` should not be caught for business recovery. **Interview angle:** Let JVM terminate or use platform watchdogs.

### 15. Assignment tie-in

Day 9 covers checked vs unchecked, `finally` ordering, custom stock exceptions, and safe parsing. **Interview angle:** Trace the assignment `try` print exercise line by line.

### 16. 60-second interview story

Classify throwable: checked for recoverable IO, unchecked for bugs, error for JVM. Use `try-with-resources` for cleanup. Wrap with causes. Parse input in small `try` blocks. Never empty catch without comment.

### 17. Satyverse drill

Write `safeParseInt` path for `null`, blank, letters. Sketch `InsufficientStockException` fields. Predict output of `try/catch/finally` sample from assignment.

### 18. Pitfall rehearsal table

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---------|--------------|-----|
| Lost root cause | swallow | rethrow with cause |
| Leaked connection | missing close | try-with-resources |
| Double fault invisible | old finally | read suppressed |

### 19. Interview angle — closing

End with **tests** proving `finally` runs and parse helpers return sentinels.

### 20. Exception kind map

| Kind | Example | Handle? |
|------|---------|---------|
| Checked | `IOException` | declare or catch |
| Unchecked | `NPE` | fix code |
| Error | `OOM` | platform action |

### 21. Logging levels versus throwables

Use `error` for faults requiring human response, `warn` for recoverable anomalies, `info` for expected business negatives that are not bugs. Never log the same stack twice at the same layer—attach correlation ids instead. **Interview angle:** Pair log fields with metrics counters keyed by exception class for SLO burn alerts.

### Production comparison table — operator triage

During a noisy incident, you separate **IOException** environmental faults from deterministic **RuntimeException** clusters before you rollback.

| Signal | Likely failure domain | First stabilising check |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Bursty **IOException** | network or disk pressure | `jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory summary` plus socket backlog dashboards |
| **RuntimeException** cluster | bad deploy or bad validation | `jstack <pid>` on a canary to read the hottest frames |
| **Error** in logs (**OutOfMemoryError**) | **JVM** heap or metaspace limits | `jmap -heap <pid>` snapshot before a controlled restart |

**Interview angle:** Tie each row to whether you retry, roll forward with a fix, or escalate to the platform team.

### 22. Fail-fast validation

Validate constructor and setter arguments before state mutation so illegal objects cannot exist; pair with exceptions that explain which rule failed. **Interview angle:** Contrast with allowing negative balances until persistence.

### 23. Parallel stream and exceptions

Uncaught exceptions from parallel streams may be wrapped in `ExecutionException` or `UncheckedIOException` depending on API—handle at reduction boundary. **Interview angle:** Mention fork-join complexity briefly.

### Exception discipline layer 1

Throwable handling is part of your API contract: callers infer retryability from types and messages. When broad catch blocks that retry deterministic IllegalArgumentException forever, reviewers demand narrower catches, structured logging with causes, and tests proving finally or try-with-resources cleanup. **Interview angle:** Name one checked exception you would declare on a DAO method and one unchecked signal for a programmer bug.

actor Caller
participant "try body" as TB

EX -> CH : dispatch matching handler
CH -> LG : log contextual message
CH -> FN : always run cleanup
alt close throws secondary fault

  AC -> EX : add suppressed throwable
else close ok
  FN -> AC : close quiet
end
FN -> Caller : resume after finally
@enduml

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Empty `catch` blocks — symptom is silent data loss in production; detect in static review; fix log or rethrow with context at boundaries.
Catching `Exception` for all retries — symptom is infinite loops on deterministic bugs; detect in metrics; fix classify checked IO vs unchecked argument faults.
Returning from `finally` — symptom is swallowed primary exceptions; detect in rare code paths; fix never return from `finally`.
Losing causes when wrapping — symptom is unreadable operator logs; detect in log aggregation; fix use cause-bearing constructors.
Using exceptions for control flow — symptom is slow hot paths and noisy logs; detect with profiling; fix use result types or optional outcomes.
Ignoring suppressed exceptions from `try-with-resources` — symptom is mystery partial writes; detect in incident forensics; fix inspect `getSuppressed()`.
Checked exceptions on APIs nobody can recover — symptom is pervasive `throws Exception` wrappers; detect in API reviews; fix use unchecked or `CompletionStage` failures.
Parsing user input without trim/null guards — symptom is spurious `NumberFormatException` in UI; detect with fuzz tests; fix centralise `safeParseInt` style helpers.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 9 assignment: checked vs unchecked, finally trace, custom stock exception, safe parsing
sections.problem:
Implement `arch.day9.Day9Exercise` covering **assignments_phase1.json** Day 9 themes (checked vs unchecked vs error, `finally` ordering lab, `InsufficientStockException`, `safeParseInt`).

Requirements:
1. Print **three** lines labelled `checked-note:`, `unchecked-note:`, and `error-note:` summarising which families must be caught/declared, which are optional, and which are JVM-level (cite at least one example type name in each line).
2. Reproduce the assignment `try/catch/finally` snippet so `main` prints **exactly** the lines `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `after` in that order (include the unreachable `after throw` println from the assignment only if you keep it commented out—do **not** print it).
3. Add static nested `InsufficientStockException` extending `RuntimeException` with **private final** `int requested` and `int available`, constructor setting `super` message **exactly** `Insufficient stock: requested ` + requested + ` but only ` + available + ` available`, and getters `getRequested()` / `getAvailable()`.
4. Add static nested `Inventory` with **private** `int stock` starting at **10** and `void withdraw(int qty)` throwing `InsufficientStockException` when `qty > stock` (no mutation in that case). In `main`, `try { new Inventory().withdraw(15); } catch` and print `stock:` + exception `getMessage()` then print `stock-available:` + `getAvailable()`.
5. Implement `static int safeParseInt(String s)` returning **-1** for `null`, empty/whitespace-only after trim, or `NumberFormatException`, else parsed int after trim—mirror the assignment behaviour.
6. After the stock demo, print **five** lines **only** the integers produced by `safeParseInt("42")`, `safeParseInt("abc")`, `safeParseInt(null)`, `safeParseInt("")`, `safeParseInt("  7  ")` so the sequence reads `42` then `-1` three times then `7`, each on its own line with no prefixes.
hints: The assignment `finally` demo must print `try` from inside the `try` block before the thrown `RuntimeException`.
Match the `InsufficientStockException` message text exactly—spacing and wording matter.
Keep `withdraw` conservative: do not subtract stock when throwing.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: Difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
conceptual.answer: For **Difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?** in the context of **Exception Handling Basics**, start from JVM internals instead of only syntax. The JVM executes operations through concrete bytecode instructions and memory models, so data representation and control decisions directly affect runtime behavior. In production, weak conceptual understanding becomes incident fuel: hidden allocations, poor branch behavior, or boundary errors increase latency, retry pressure, and GC churn. Diagnose with real tooling: use `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` for memory posture, `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 10` for GC pressure trends, and `javap -c` to inspect what bytecode is actually emitted for critical logic. Version awareness matters too: Java 8/11/17/21 differ in defaults and runtime optimizations, so migration testing must validate both correctness and performance. Keep the explanation fresher-friendly by pairing internal mechanism with one simple concrete example, but keep staff-level depth by connecting every concept to an operational consequence and an observable metric.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how do **exception choices** affect **production** incidents?
followUps.answer: For checked vs unchecked, you see masked root causes after empty catches, retry loops amplifying deterministic bugs, and leaked connections when finally blocks are skipped mentally. You add structured logging with causes, classify faults before retry policies, and enforce try-with-resources in reviews. You verify with chaos tests that resources close under failure.

followUps.question: What **trap** might follow your answer?
followUps.answer: Interviewers may ask about `Thread.stop` history, `Error` catching in frameworks, or Kotlin checked exception differences. You respond with JVM contract clarity, platform watchdog responsibilities, and boundary translation layers. You close with one test asserting suppressed exceptions surface when a close fails after a write error.

seniorScenario.question: Payments service logs ‘success’ but ledger missing—exceptions swallowed?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I stabilize impact first for the Exception Handling Basics path by checking user-facing error rate, p95/p99 latency, and rollback options before touching code. I collect runtime evidence with `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, `jstack <pid>`, and a short `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 20` sample so decisions are data-driven within the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **Payments service logs ‘success’ but ledger missing—exceptions swallowed?**, the usual root cause is mismatch between intended Java behavior and actual JVM execution characteristics (allocation shape, bytecode branch paths, or boundary assumptions). Under realistic load, this mismatch manifests as exception spikes, throughput collapse, or GC pause amplification that may not appear in narrow local tests.

**Fix:** I apply a narrow correction with explicit guardrails: harden edge conditions, remove avoidable boxing/copying in hot paths, and add deterministic fallback logic where needed. I verify with targeted tests, canary rollout metrics, `javap -c` spot checks for emitted bytecode, and post-deploy SLO comparisons to confirm the change improves behavior without side effects.

**Prevention:** I codify prevention in process and architecture: add checklist rules for this topic in code review, enforce boundary/performance tests in CI, and track dashboards that correlate allocation rate, exception counts, and latency. I also document Java 8/11/17/21 migration notes so future upgrades treat runtime behavior as a first-class validation target, not just compile success.

wrongAnswers: `finally` never runs if an exception is thrown in `try`.
It is good practice to catch `Throwable` in every service method.
Suppressed exceptions discard the primary failure entirely.
`NumberFormatException` is a checked exception you must declare.
Empty catch blocks are preferred to reduce log noise in production.
Returning from `finally` is the cleanest way to override a `try` result.
Custom exceptions should never include extra fields for context.

options.B: Choose JVM-aware behavior with observability and safe rollout
options.C: Ignore edge cases and rely on retries
options.D: Assume all Java versions behave identically

sections.title: Cheatsheet — exceptions and failure handling
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Checked | extends Exception not Runtime | "Caller must plan recovery." |
| Unchecked | extends RuntimeException | "Signals bug or rule break." |
| Error | JVM serious fault | "Do not catch for business logic." |
| finally | always runs | "Cleanup before return paths." |
| try-with-resources | AutoCloseable | "Suppress close faults properly." |
| Cause chain | wrap with cause | "Keep root stack for ops." |
| Swallow | empty catch | "Forbidden without documented reason." |
| parseInt | NumberFormatException | "Guard user input." |
| Custom | domain type | "Metrics + messages beat strings." |
| Multi-catch | order specific first | "Compiler enforces reachability." |
| Rethrow | add context | "Translate JDBC to domain at boundary." |
| Logging | once at edge | "Avoid duplicate stack spam." |
| Retry | classify fault | "Never retry programmer bugs blindly." |
| Suppressed | getSuppressed | "Close can fail too—log both." |
| throws clause | API contract | "Changing throws is semver-sensitive." |
